---
title: "Research Project Template"
subtitle: "A subtitle for your project."
description: "[WIP] Starter structure for research project notes, data, analysis, methods, and references."
author: "Firstname Lastname"

# -- Page behavior --
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
number-sections: false
code-fold: true          # all code chunks collapsed by default; reader can expand
code-tools: false        # hide the global code tools menu
fig-align: center
execute:
  freeze: true           # only re-run when you explicitly render this page
  warning: false

# -- Lightbox for zoomable images --
lightbox: true
---

```{=html}
<!-- ============================================================
     This file is a feature showcase, not real content.
     Replace placeholder text/code with your actual work.
     ============================================================ -->
```

## Overview

```{=html}
<!--
  Research Project Overview Component
  Structure based on Booth et al. (2016), The Craft of Research, 4th ed.
  Sections follow the book's progression: Interest → Topic → Questions → Significance → Problem → Hypothesis → Sources
-->
```

### Research Interest

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="What is a research interest?"}
A research interest is the raw starting material for a project. This is the thing you're curious about *before* you've scoped, focused, or formalized it. It is deliberately pre-academic. You are not making a claim, asking a question, or proposing a study. You are identifying the **itch**. The fascination. The puzzle. The nagging observation that makes you want to know more.

The book's framing: "a research topic is an interest stated specifically enough for you to imagine becoming a local expert on it." The interest comes **before** that. It is the broader domain of fascination from which a topic will be carved. Many researchers have more interests than they can pursue; the challenge is not generating them but selecting among them.

**Good interests often start as:** things you find yourself explaining to others unprompted, phenomena that don't fit the explanations you've been taught, patterns you notice across systems, questions that keep recurring across your reading, or observations from your own empirical work that surprise you.

**Write your interest as a simple declarative statement of fascination, not a research question.** Example: "I'm fascinated by how individual ants with tiny brains collectively solve problems that seem to require centralized intelligence." Not yet: "How do ant colonies make decisions?" which comes later.

*(Booth et al. 2016, §3.1)*
:::

<!-- Write your research interest here -->

### Broad Research Topic

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="What is a broad research topic?"}
A broad research topic takes your interest and names a domain of inquiry. The goal is to have a broad topicbut it is still too wide to guide productive research. At this stage, you can state your topic in four or five words: "division of labor in ants," "the evolution of cooperation," "gene regulation in development." The book warns that a topic this broad will "intimidate you with the task of finding, much less reading, even a fraction of the sources available."

The purpose of writing it down explicitly is diagnostic: if your topic is this broad, you know you haven't focused yet, and you can see what work remains. A broad topic is a *starting place* (the word "topic" comes from *topos*, Greek for "place"), not a destination. You will head off from here in a particular direction.

**Test:** Can you restate your topic as a full sentence that makes an interesting claim? If the sentence just restates itself ("The evolution of cooperation has a history"), you're still too broad.

*(Booth et al. 2016, §3.2)*
:::

<!-- Write your broad topic here -->

### Focused Research Topic

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="What is a focused research topic?"}
You narrow a broad topic into a focused one by adding words derived from **verbs that express actions or relationships**: *conflict*, *influence*, *cause*, *contribute*, *change*, *develop*, *shape*, *constrain*. A static topic ("caste determination in ants") becomes a focused one when it contains dynamic, relational language ("the *influence* of queen presence on larval developmental *trajectories* during the 3rd-instar commitment window in *Temnothorax*").

The book's key test: **restate your focused topic as a full sentence.** If that sentence makes a claim a reader might find interesting — even if thin at this stage — you have a viable topic. If it is merely a tautology ("Caste determination exists in social insects"), you need more focusing.

**How to focus:**

- Add **action concepts**: What is conflicting, changing, influencing, developing, constraining, or emerging?
- Add **specificity**: Which system, which species, which time period, which population, which level of organization?
- Add **comparison or contrast**: Between what and what?

**Caution:** Don't narrow so far that you can't find information or that the question becomes trivial. The book warns against both extremes — too much available information (unfocused) and too little (over-narrowed).

*(Booth et al. 2016, §3.2)*
:::

<!-- Write your focused topic here -->

### Research Questions

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="How to generate and evaluate research questions"}
The best way to begin working on a focused topic is **not** to find all the information you can, but to formulate questions that direct you to just the information you need. Without a specific question worth asking, you cannot offer a specific answer worth supporting, and without an answer to support, you cannot select from all available data just those relevant to your case.

#### Systematic question generation

Ask questions across these categories (the point is to stimulate thinking, not to keep the categories straight):

- **History / Development:** How did this come into being? What came before it? How and why has it changed over time? What might come after it?
- **Structure / Composition:** How does it fit into a larger system? How do its parts relate to each other? What role does it play in a higher-level process?
- **Categorization:** How can it be grouped into kinds? How does it compare and contrast with similar things?
- **Negative inversions:** Turn positive questions negative. Why does X *not* occur? How does A *not* differ from B? What features are typically *not* significant?
- **Speculative / Counterfactual:** What if this didn't exist? What if it were placed in a new context? How would things be different?
- **Source-driven questions:** Build on agreement — can you extend a source's claim to new cases, support it with new evidence, or apply an analogous analysis? Build on disagreement — does a source mistake a causal relationship, oversimplify a category, or ignore a perspective?

#### Evaluating questions

Not all questions are equally good. Avoid questions whose answers are:

- **Settled fact** you could just look up (prefer *how* and *why* over *who*, *what*, *when*, *where*)
- **Purely speculative** with no imaginable data that could settle them
- **Dead ends** that don't connect to any larger issue worth understanding

Look for questions whose answers would make you think about the topic in a **new way**. Then try to **combine** small questions into larger ones that give direction to your research.

*(Booth et al. 2016, §3.3)*
:::

<!-- Write your research questions here -->

### Significance ("So What?")

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Why this is the pivotal section"}
This is the most important section in the overview — and the hardest to fill in. Once you have a question that holds your interest, you must pose a tougher one about it: **So what?** Beyond your own interest in its answer, why would others think it a question worth asking?

The book provides a three-step formula that is the central organizing device of the entire research process:

> **Step 1 — Topic:** I am studying / working on \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
>
> **Step 2 — Question:** because I want to find out who/what/when/where/whether/why/how \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_
>
> **Step 3 — Significance:** in order to help my reader understand \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

**Step 1 → Step 2** is the transition from data collector to researcher. **Step 2 → Step 3** is the transition from someone with a question to someone with a *problem that a community cares about*. Without Step 3, the worst response your work can receive is not "I disagree" but **"I don't care."**

Think of it this way: *What will be lost if you don't answer your question?* How will not answering it keep us from understanding something else better than we do?

**It is normal** not to be able to fill in Step 3 at the outset of a project — many researchers can't complete it until they finish a first draft. But you must keep working toward it, and you should revisit this section regularly. The book is explicit: "you make no mistake beginning your research without a good answer to that third question — but you face a problem when you finish your research without having thought through those three steps at all."

**Test your progress:** Can you explain your three-step sentence to a colleague, a friend, a roommate? Even if Step 3 is tentative, articulating the attempt reveals where you are and where you still need to go.

*(Booth et al. 2016, §3.4)*
:::

**Step 1 — Topic:** I am studying ...

**Step 2 — Question:** ... because I want to find out ...

**Step 3 — Significance:** ... in order to help my reader understand ...

### Problem Structure

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Transforming a question into a research problem"}
A question becomes a research *problem* when you can articulate not just what you don't know, but what **consequence** that ignorance has — what larger, more important question you can't answer because of this gap. The book defines every problem as having two parts:

1.  **Condition:** some version of not knowing or not understanding something
2.  **Consequence:** a lack of understanding that keeps us from understanding something *else* even more significant

For conceptual/academic problems, both the condition and the consequence are abstract (unlike practical problems, where the cost is tangible). The condition is your research question from Step 2 above. The consequence is the answer to "So what?" from Step 3. But here you make the relationship between them **explicit and structural:**

> If we cannot answer Q1 \[your question\], then we cannot answer Q2 \[a more important question\].

The two questions must relate in two ways: (a) the answer to Q1 helps answer Q2, and (b) Q2 is more important than Q1.

#### Practical vs. Conceptual problems

Most academic research addresses **conceptual problems** — the "cost" is a gap in understanding, not a tangible harm in the world. If your research also has practical implications, the book advises: formulate the project as pure research first (Steps 1–3 addressing understanding), then add practical application as an **optional Step 4**. Don't artificially bolt practical significance onto conceptual work to make it seem more important — readers will see through it. The book provides a test:

> (a) If my readers want to achieve \[practical goal from Step 4\], (b) would they think they could do it if they found out \[answer to your question from Step 2\]?

If the answer is "probably not," the practical connection is a stretch. Present your conceptual significance honestly and save the practical application for your conclusion.

#### Finding problems

- **In your sources:** Look for contradictions, inconsistencies, incomplete explanations, and unsupported assumptions. Many journal articles end with paragraphs about open questions and ideas for further research — carve out a piece of one.
- **In your own drafts:** Your best thinking often appears in the last pages you write. If you arrive at an unanticipated claim, ask yourself what question it might answer — you may have solved a problem you haven't yet posed.
- **Through conversation:** Talk to colleagues, advisors, and collaborators. What would they do with your answer? What new questions might it raise?

*(Booth et al. 2016, §4.1–4.4)*
:::

**Condition (Q1):** We do not know / understand ...

**Consequence (Q2):** ... which prevents us from understanding ...

**Problem type:** ☐ Conceptual (pure) ☐ Conceptual with practical application ☐ Applied

**If applied — Step 4 (Practical application):**

### Working Hypothesis / Elevator Story

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Why you need a working answer before you have all the evidence"}
The book describes an "elevator story" — a short summary of your project that you could give someone on the way to a meeting. It should include: (1) your research question, (2) your best guess at an answer, and (3) the kind of evidence you expect to use to support it.

This is not a commitment to a conclusion. It is a **working hypothesis** — what the philosopher C. S. Peirce called a "hypothesis on probation." Its purpose is threefold:

- It gives **direction** to your search for evidence (without a tentative answer, you don't know what data to look for)
- It makes your project **communicable** to others (advisors, lab members, collaborators)
- It is **revisable** — you will update it as evidence accumulates, and that revision *is* the intellectual progress of the project

The book is explicit that research is not a linear march from question to answer: "it's more like zigzagging up and down a rocky hill through overgrown woods, sometimes in a fog, searching for something you won't recognize until you see it." The working hypothesis is your compass bearing, not your destination.

**Write this as a single paragraph.** Revise it regularly — each revision is a snapshot of your evolving understanding.

*(Booth et al. 2016, Part II Prologue; §3.4)*
:::

<!-- Write your working hypothesis / elevator story here -->

### Sources & Problem-Finding

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="How to engage sources at the overview stage"}
At this stage, you are **not** conducting a comprehensive literature review. You are reading to accomplish two things: (1) find and refine your research problem, and (2) identify the kinds of evidence your readers will expect.

#### Three kinds of sources

- **Primary sources:** The "raw data" or evidence you will use to develop, test, and justify your claim. In behavioral ecology, these are your own observations, experimental results, datasets. What counts as primary varies by field.
- **Secondary sources:** Peer-reviewed books and articles based on primary sources, intended for scholarly audiences. These constitute the **conversation** you are joining. The book warns: if you cite only tertiary sources in a scholarly argument, "you will mark yourself as either a novice or an outsider."
- **Tertiary sources:** Syntheses for general readers — textbooks, encyclopedias, review articles aimed at broad audiences. Useful for orientation but limited: they can oversimplify and become outdated. Use them to get your bearings, then move to secondary sources.

**Important:** These categories are relative to your project. A journal article is normally a secondary source, but it becomes primary if your research concerns the author, the field's methods, or its intellectual history.

#### Reading for a problem

Don't read randomly — read deliberately to find problems. Look for:

- **Creative agreement:** Can you extend a source's claim to new cases? Offer new or stronger evidence? Apply their framework to a new system?
- **Creative disagreement:** Does a source mistake a causal relationship? Oversimplify a category? Ignore a relevant perspective? Assume something is true without evidence? Claim something has a feature it doesn't?

The book lists specific "moves" for finding disagreements: contradictions of kind (it's not that type of thing), part-whole contradictions (that's not how the parts relate), developmental contradictions (that's not how it changes over time), causal contradictions (that's not what causes it), and contradictions of perspective (a different framework reveals something new).

#### Evaluating sources

Key criteria: Is it peer-reviewed? Is the author reputable in the field? Is it current by your field's standards? Does it have notes and a bibliography? Has it been frequently cited? For any source on a contested topic, check who funded it.

#### Following trails

Use **bibliographic trails** (follow citations backward from a useful source) and **citation indexing** (follow citations forward — who has cited this source since it was published?). The combination builds a network of sources far more efficiently than keyword searching alone.

*(Booth et al. 2016, §5.1–5.4, §6.3)*
:::

#### Key sources identified so far

<!-- Use this space to track sources that are shaping your problem, not as an exhaustive bibliography -->

| Source | Type | Role in your argument |
|--------|------|-----------------------|
|        |      |                       |
|        |      |                       |
|        |      |                       |

#### Open questions from your reading

<!-- What contradictions, gaps, or incomplete explanations have you found? -->

## Data

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Quarto feature: callout blocks

These come in five flavors -- `note`, `tip`, `warning`, `caution`, `important`. They can be collapsible (`collapse="true"`) and support full markdown inside.
:::

### Loading & Summarizing Data

Code chunks execute at render time. `code-fold: true` in the YAML means the code is hidden by default but expandable -- readers see the output, researchers can inspect the code.

```{python}
#| label: fig-growth-trajectories
#| fig-cap: "Simulated larval growth trajectories by treatment. Replace with real data."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 5

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)
days = np.arange(0, 30)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

# Simulated trajectories -- replace with actual DB query
for i in range(12):
    treatment = "queenright" if i < 6 else "queenless"
    color = "#2d6a4f" if treatment == "queenright" else "#d4a373"
    k = np.random.uniform(0.08, 0.15)
    L_inf = np.random.uniform(2.5, 4.0) if treatment == "queenright" else np.random.uniform(1.8, 3.0)
    noise = np.random.normal(0, 0.05, len(days))
    length = L_inf * (1 - np.exp(-k * days)) + noise
    ax.plot(days, length, color=color, alpha=0.6, linewidth=1.5,
            label=treatment if i in [0, 6] else None)

ax.set_xlabel("Day")
ax.set_ylabel("Body length (mm)")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
ax.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Cross-reference: the growth trajectories in @fig-growth-trajectories suggest treatment effects on asymptotic size.

### Inline Computed Values

Quarto can embed computed values directly in prose:

```{python}
#| echo: false
n_total = 128
n_per_treatment = 64
imaging_days = 30
total_images = n_total * imaging_days * 24
```

The experiment will generate approximately `{python} f"{total_images:,}"` images over `{python} imaging_days` days -- this number updates automatically if you change the parameters above.

## Analysis

### Growth Model

Quarto renders LaTeX natively. Useful for documenting your statistical models alongside the code that fits them:

Individual growth follows a von Bertalanffy curve:

$$L(t) = L_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k(t - t_0)}\right)$$ {#eq-vonbert}

where $L_\infty$ is the asymptotic length, $k$ is the growth rate constant, and $t_0$ is the theoretical age at zero length. See @eq-vonbert.

The treatment effect enters through $L_\infty$:

$$L_{\infty,i} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{queenless}_i + u_{\text{colony}(i)}$$ {#eq-treatment}

with colony as a random intercept (@eq-treatment). This is a mixed-effects nonlinear regression -- `nlme::nlme` or `scipy.optimize.curve_fit` per individual followed by a linear model on the extracted parameters.

### Fitted Results

```{python}
#| label: fig-parameter-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Comparison of fitted growth parameters between treatments."
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "Asymptotic length"
#|   - "Growth rate constant"
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| fig-width: 4
#| fig-height: 4

# --- Quarto feature: sub-figures with shared caption and layout control ---

# Panel A: L_inf by treatment
fig1, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 4))
qr_linf = np.random.normal(3.2, 0.3, 64)
ql_linf = np.random.normal(2.4, 0.4, 64)
parts = ax1.violinplot([qr_linf, ql_linf], positions=[1, 2], showmedians=True)
for pc in parts['bodies']:
    pc.set_alpha(0.6)
ax1.set_xticks([1, 2])
ax1.set_xticklabels(["Queenright", "Queenless"])
ax1.set_ylabel("$L_\\infty$ (mm)")
ax1.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Panel B: k by treatment
fig2, ax2 = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 4))
qr_k = np.random.normal(0.12, 0.02, 64)
ql_k = np.random.normal(0.10, 0.025, 64)
parts = ax2.violinplot([qr_k, ql_k], positions=[1, 2], showmedians=True)
for pc in parts['bodies']:
    pc.set_alpha(0.6)
ax2.set_xticks([1, 2])
ax2.set_xticklabels(["Queenright", "Queenless"])
ax2.set_ylabel("$k$ (day$^{-1}$)")
ax2.spines[['top', 'right']].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Reference sub-figures individually: @fig-parameter-comparison-1 shows the asymptotic length distributions, while @fig-parameter-comparison-2 shows growth rate constants.

::: {.callout-warning collapse="true"}
## Quarto feature: margin notes

You can place content in the page margin with the `.column-margin` class. Useful for side-commentary, small reference figures, or annotation without interrupting the main flow. Add `column: margin` to a code chunk's YAML to push its output to the margin.
:::

## Methods

### CV Pipeline

Describe your SAM-based segmentation, morphometric extraction, etc.

::: column-margin
**Pipeline version:** `v0.3.2`\
**Last validated:** 2026-06-15
:::

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-pipeline
%%| fig-cap: "Image processing pipeline from raw frame to morphometric measurement."

flowchart TD
    A[Raw frame<br/>RPi camera] --> B[Background subtraction]
    B --> C[SAM segmentation]
    C --> D{Mask QC}
    D -->|pass| E[Morphometric extraction]
    D -->|fail| F[Manual correction tool]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Spline-based length]
    E --> H[Ellipse-based area]
    G --> I[(Measurements DB)]
    H --> I
```

### Protocol Details

::: column-margin
Testing column margin stuff
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Quarto feature: collapsible callouts

Lengthy protocols, equipment lists, or troubleshooting notes can go inside collapsible callouts. The reader sees the header; they expand only if needed. This keeps the page scannable without losing detail.

**Example protocol step:** Aspirate 5 workers from source colony using a modified 15 mL Falcon tube aspirator. Transfer to micronest arena within 30 seconds to minimize stress handling. Confirm 5 workers visually before sealing.
:::

### Linking to AntSciHub

Reference specific video observations inline:

> The larval secretion event in colony 4, nest 17 was first observed at hour 342. See the raw footage: [AntSciHub link](https://antscihub.org/v/colony04/nest17/t342).

```{=html}
<!-- ============================================================
     END OF TEMPLATE SECTIONS
     ============================================================ -->
```

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Reproducibility Info

**Source:** [`Research Project Template.qmd`](https://github.com/soulsynapse/antscihub/blob/main/Templates/Research%20Project%20Template.qmd) \| **Render path:** GitHub Actions builds and deploys the site from `main`
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

Add `bibliography: references.bib` to the YAML and cite entries with `[@key]` once this notebook starts using literature references.
